<!-- Topic 1: Object-Oriented Programming -->
<!-- Total slides: 4 -->

# Object-Oriented Programming

---

## Procedural vs. object-oriented {.smaller}

+ **Procedural**: organize code around *actions* — functions that receive data and produce results.
+ **Object-oriented**: organize code around *things* — objects that own their own data and know how to operate on it.

::: notes
Total slides: 4
Bank account example: procedural thinks in verbs (withdraw, deposit, transfer); OOP thinks in nouns (Customer, Account, Money). Same program, completely different organizing lens. This paradigm shift is the core of what students are stepping into.
[Graphic suggestion: Two-column diagram — left shows person silhouette with action arrows labeled withdraw/deposit/transfer; right shows same person surrounded by Customer, Account, Money objects circling her.]
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## The class is the blueprint {.smaller}

+ A **class** defines a new type — it specifies what data it holds and what operations it supports.
+ An **object** is one specific instance created from that class — like a cookie cut from a cutter.

::: notes
Cookie cutter analogy: one class definition (the cutter) stamps out as many objects (cookies) as you need. Each object is independent — same shape, its own data.
[Graphic suggestion: Cookie cutter labeled "the class" pointing to two cookie shapes labeled "the objects."]
:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## One class, many objects {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
class Car {
    // members defined here
};

Car mustang;   // Green, Ford, Mustang, Gasoline
Car prius;     // Red, Toyota, Prius, Electric
Car golf;      // Blue, Volkswagen, Golf, Diesel
```

::: notes
Same class, three distinct objects — each holding different data. This mirrors what students already know from scalars: int x; int y; is the same type with different values. The class is declared once; objects are created as needed.
[Graphic suggestion: Car class at top with arrows down to three car icons (green/red/blue), each labeled with make/model/fuel.]
:::

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Summary {.smaller}

+ OOP organizes programs around objects — things that own their own data and behavior.
+ Declare a class once; create as many objects as needed — just like any other type.

<!-- Slide 4 -->
